labelling_rule: A symbol is a real extension point when the package's own documentation presents it as something a user implements, subclasses, or registers against - not merely calls. Deliberately independent of public-export status, which is the candidate signal under test. See benchmarks/README.md.

pluggy.note: Defines its public API inside underscore-prefixed modules and re-exports from __init__. Any heuristic penalising private module paths must be measured here first.

extension_points.justification: Docs 'Plugin management': users instantiate PluginManager and register their plugin objects against it. It is the entry point of the whole plugin lifecycle.
Docs 'Specs': a host application defines its hook specifications by decorating functions with a HookspecMarker instance.
Docs 'Impls': a plugin author marks hook implementations with a HookimplMarker instance. This is how a pluggy plugin is written.
Docs 'Implementing Custom Types': the documented way to add a parameter type is to subclass ParamType and implement convert().
Docs 'Custom Commands': a custom command class is supplied via the cls= argument to the command decorator.
Docs 'Custom Groups' / lazy command loading: subclassing Group is the documented way to control how subcommands are discovered and loaded.
Docs 'Parameters': custom parameter behaviour is documented via cls= on option/argument decorators, pointing at Parameter subclasses.

sqlalchemy.note: Deliberately partial. SQLAlchemy documents more seams than are labelled here; recall against a partial list is still a valid regression signal.

extension_points.justification: Docs 'Augmenting Existing Types': TypeDecorator is the documented base class for wrapping an existing type with custom bind/result processing.
Docs 'Creating New Types': UserDefinedType is the documented base for defining an entirely new database type.
Docs 'Defining Custom SQL Constructs': user-defined SQL functions are created by subclassing FunctionElement (commonly via GenericFunction) and registering a compiler.
Docs 'Developing new Dialects': third-party database drivers integrate with SQLAlchemy by implementing the Dialect interface.
Docs 'Custom DDL': custom DDL constructs are defined by subclassing ExecutableDDLElement and attaching a compiler.
